conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: I used to think test-driven development (TDD) was a complete religion pushed by purists. Writing tests before code felt like a waste of engineering time.

conversation.speaker: assistant
conversation.text: It can definitely feel slow when you are trying to rapidly iterate on a feature.

conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: But after debugging a massive regression that cost us thousands, I completely converted. I firmly believed that 100% strict TDD and mandatory 95% test coverage were non-negotiable for producing professional software.

conversation.speaker: assistant
conversation.text: High coverage requirements definitely give teams more confidence during refactoring.

conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: Actually, I realized that high coverage just leads to developers writing meaningless tests to satisfy the CI gate. I've abandoned that dogmatic view. I now strongly believe that testing should focus entirely on integration boundaries and critical user journeys, completely ignoring unit test coverage metrics.

query: What is the user's current philosophy regarding software testing and code coverage?
